context.pr_title: feat: Switch to enum queue mode, add 'replace' mode.
context.pr_body:
Follow up to https://github.com/Acly/krita-ai-diffusion/pull/1879

Kind of janky because of the fact that job queue tasks are split up before the client- I make clear-queue a property of the first job.

Not sure if the enum is in the right place.

Tentatively planning to implement ctrl-click as "Alternate queue mode".

agents.name: analysis_agent
agents.system_prompt:
You are an Analysis Agent specialized in Pull Request analysis. ## Project Context (MASCA Analysis) ## Project Analysis ### README

Generative AI for Krita

✨[Features](#features) | ⭳ [Download](https://github.com/Acly/krita-ai-diffusion/releases/latest) | 🛠️[Installation](https://docs.interstice.cloud/installation) | 🎞️ [Video](https://youtu.be/Ly6USRwTHe0) | 🖼️[Gallery](#gallery) | 📖[User Guide](https://docs.interstice.cloud) | 💬[Discussion](https://github.com/Acly/krita-ai-diffusion/discussions) | 🗣️[Discord](https://discord.gg/pWyzHfHHhU) This is a plugin to use generative AI in image painting and editing workflows from within Krita. Visit [**www.interstice.cloud**](https://www.interstice.cloud) for an introduction. Learn how to install and use it on [**docs.interstice.cloud**](https://docs.interstice.cloud). The main goals of this project are: * **Precision and Control.** Creating entire images from text can be unpredictable. To get the result you envision, you can restrict generation to selections, refine existing content with a variable degree of strength, focus text on image regions, and guide generation with reference images, sketches, line art, depth maps, and more. * **Workflow Integration.** Most image generation tools focus heavily on AI parameters. This project aims to be an unobtrusive tool that integrates and synergizes with image editing workflows in Krita. Draw, paint, edit and generate seamlessly without worrying about resolution and technical details. * **Local, Open, Free.** We are committed to open source models. Customize presets, bring your own models, and run everything local on your hardware. Cloud generation is also available to get started quickly without heavy investment. [![Watch video demo](media/screenshot-video-preview.webp)](https://youtu.be/Ly6USRwTHe0 "Watch video demo") ## Features * **Inpainting**: Use selections for generative fill, expand, to add or remove objects * **Live Painting**: Let AI interpret your canvas in real time for immediate feedback. [Watch Video](https://youtu.be/AF2VyqSApjA?si=Ve5uQJWcNOATtABU) * **Upscaling**: Upscale and enrich images to 4k, 8k and beyond without running out of memory. * **Diffusion Models**: Supports Stable Diffusion 1.5, and XL. Partial support for [Flux](https://github.com/Acly/krita-ai-diffusion/discussions/1176) and SD3. * **ControlNet**: Scribble, Line art, Canny edge, Pose, Depth, Normals, Segmentation, +more * **IP-Adapter**: Reference images, Style and composition transfer, Face swap * **Regions**: Assign individual text descriptions to image areas defined by layers. * **Job Queue**: Queue and cancel generation jobs while working on your image. * **History**: Preview results and browse previous generations and prompts at any time. * **Strong Defaults**: Versatile default style presets allow for a streamlined UI. * **Customization**: Create your own presets - custom checkpoints, LoRA, samplers and more. ## Getting Started See the [Plugin Install ## Your Task When you receive a PR title and body, you must: 1. **Analyze the request**: Understand what the PR wants to achieve 2. **Explore the code**: Use tools to navigate base_project and find relevant files/functions 3. **Identify targets**: Determine exactly which files and functions need to be modified 4. **Optimize MASCA**: Produce a version of the MASCA analysis focused on the specific PR ## Available Tools - `read_base_project_file(file_path)`: Read a file from the project - `list_base_project_directory(directory, pattern, recursive)`: List files/folders ## Required Output You must produce a structured output with: - `pr_title`: PR title - `pr_body`: PR body - `masca_optimized`: MASCA optimized for this specific PR - `files_to_modify`: List of files to modify with motivation - `functions_to_modify`: List of functions to modify with motivation - `analysis_summary`: Summary of your analysis ## Guidelines - Explore the code before drawing conclusions - Search for files with names relevant to the PR - Identify specific functions, not just generic files - Be precise with paths (relative to base_project) - For function names use the format "ClassName.method_name" for methods
agents.input_prompt:
Analyze this Pull Request:

## Title
feat: Switch to enum queue mode, add 'replace' mode.

## Description
Follow up to https://github.com/Acly/krita-ai-diffusion/pull/1879

Kind of janky because of the fact that job queue tasks are split up before the client- I make clear-queue a property of the first job.

Not sure if the enum is in the right place.

Tentatively planning to implement ctrl-click as "Alternate queue mode".

Explore the source code to identify the files and functions to modify.

arguments.reason: Inspect current job queue handling to identify how queue state is managed and how to introduce an enum queue mode.
arguments.expected_information: Where queue mode is defined, how jobs are enqueued, and how props like 'clear queue' are passed.

tool_calls.result:
### File Overview
This file defines the core client interface and data structures used to interact with an AI diffusion server, including job queuing, model/resource management, style/arch resolution, and related utilities. It includes the abstract Client interface (enqueue, listen, clear_queue), various event/data types, and helper functions used by the agent to reason about queue state and resources.

### Architecture
- Data models and events: Defines enums, named tuples, and simple classes (ClientEvent, TextOutput, SharedWorkflow, ClientOutput, ClientMessage, etc.) to represent events, outputs, and messages flowing between the client and server.
- User and device info: Provides User, DeviceInfo, and related parsing/notification mechanisms to track user state and hardware capabilities.
- Resource and model management: ClientModels and ModelDict collectively model available resources (e.g., upscalers, loras, vae) and provide filtered access tailored to a given architecture or workflow.
- Client interface: The abstract Client class prescribes the lifecycle and capabilities (connect, enqueue, listen, interrupt, clear_queue, translate, etc.) that concrete implementations must provide.
- Helpers for architecture/style: resolve_arch and filter_supported_styles help select appropriate architectures and styles given the client’s capabilities and available checkpoints.
- Workflow/resource helpers: loras_to_upload and related constants manage the uploading/validation of LoRA models required by a workflow.
- Error handling: MissingResources encapsulates missing model/custom-node requirements per architecture.
Overall, the file centralizes how the agent will enqueue and manage jobs and assets, how queue-related state is represented, and how to resolve the right models for a given workflow.

### Functions & Classes

- ClientEvent — enumeration of client-side events (progress, finished, interrupted, error, connected, disconnected, queued, upload, published, output, payment_required). Importance: defines the lifecycle and status signals the client can emit, including a queued state relevant to queue management.

- TextOutput — NamedTuple with fields key, name, text, mime. Importance: represents textual outputs produced by a workflow (e.g., logs or notes) that can be surfaced to the UI or downstream consumers.

- SharedWorkflow — NamedTuple with publisher and workflow. Importance: supports sharing a workflow/workflow state between clients or sessions.

- ClientOutput — alias for dict | SharedWorkflow | TextOutput. Importance: union type for various kinds of outputs a client might return after processing.

- ClientMessage — NamedTuple with event, job_id, progress, images, result, error. Importance: core carrier of state updates from the client, including progress and results for a given job.

- User — class (QObject, ObservableProperties) with id, name, images_generated, credits, and associated signals. Importance: tracks user-related metrics that may influence queue behavior (e.g., credits).

- DeviceInfo — NamedTuple(type, name, vram). Static parse(data: dict) to extract device capabilities from a server response. Importance: helps determine hardware constraints for queuing decisions (e.g., available VRAM).

- MissingResources — Exception subclass storing missing: dict[Arch, list[ResourceId]] | list[CustomNode]. Methods: __str__, get(arch). Importance: communicates what is missing to satisfy a workflow or model requirement, impacting enqueue viability.

- CheckpointInfo — NamedTuple(filename, arch, format=FileFormat.checkpoint). Property name, static deduce_from_filename(filename). Importance: encapsulates checkpoint metadata and supports inference of architecture from filenames; used to select compatible models during enqueue.

- ClientModels — class that collects names of AI models the client has access to. Attributes include checkpoints, vae, loras, upscalers, resources, node_inputs. Methods:
  - resource(kind, identifier, arch) — retrieve a model/resource by kind/identifier/arch, raising if not found.
  - find(id) — locate a resource by ResourceId with fallbacks (e.g., epsilon/Illu variants, universal).
  - arch_of(checkpoint) — infer arch from a given checkpoint.
  - for_arch(arch) / upscale (and related properties) — provide filtered views for a given architecture.
  - default_upscaler — convenience accessor.
  Importance: central repository of what models/resources are available; used to validate enqueue requests and resolve the correct assets for a job.

- ModelDict — class providing access to a filtered list of models matching a certain base model. Members:
  - __init__(models, kind, arch)
  - __getitem__(key)
  - find(key, allow_universal=False)
  - for_version(arch)
  - text_encoder, clip_vision, upscale, control, ip_adapter, inpaint, lora, vae, fooocus_inpaint, all, node_inputs, has_te_vae
  Importance: facilitates retrieving the correct resource names/identifiers for a given kind and architecture when assembling a workflow for enqueue.

- TranslationPackage — NamedTuple(code, name). Static factory methods from_dict and from_list. Importance: supports localization/internationalization planning for the UI client.

- ClientFeatures — NamedTuple with ip_adapter, translation, languages, max_upload_size, max_control_layers, wave_speed, gguf. Importance: describes capabilities the client advertises to servers, which can influence enqueue constraints and UI options.

- Client (ABC) — abstract interface defining:
  - url, models, device_info attributes
  - connect(url, access_token) -> Client
  - enqueue(work: WorkflowInput, front: bool = False) -> str
  - listen() -> AsyncGenerator[ClientMessage, Any]
  - interrupt()
  - clear_queue()
  - refresh(), translate(text, lang), disconnect()
  - user (property), missing_resources (property)
  - supports_arch(arch)
  - features, performance_settings properties
  - async context manager methods __aenter__ and __aexit__
  Importance: central contract for any server/client implementation; enqueue and clear_queue are directly tied to queue management, while listen/ClientMessage provide queue state updates.

- resolve_arch(style: Style, client: Client | ClientModels | None = None) — determines the architecture to use based on a Style (and possibly an available Client or its models). Handles preferred checkpoints, architecture inference, and sdxl-like overrides. Importance: ensures the correct model/architecture is selected for a given workflow, which is critical for queue correctness and asset resolution.

- filter_supported_styles(styles: Iterable[Style], client: Client | None = None) — filters a collection of styles to those supported by the client (and with valid checkpoints) if a client is provided. Importance: used to present only viable styling options that can be queued.

- loras_to_upload(workflow: WorkflowInput, client_models: ClientModels) — iterates required LoRA assets for a workflow, validating presence, storage IDs, and existence on disk; yields local lora files to upload. Importance: ensures that all LoRA assets needed by an enqueueable workflow are present and uploadable; this directly affects queue readiness.

- _lcm_loras — list containing specific LoRA names related to LCM. Importance: used by loras_to_upload for legacy/incompatibility checks.

- _lcm_warning — warning string about LCM no longer being supported by the server. Importance: user-facing guidance within the LoRA upload process.

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
- Queue state and mode: The file defines the core queue interaction points via Client.enqueue and Client.clear_queue, plus the ClientEvent.queued event, which are central to understanding how the current system represents and manipulates queue state.
- Queue mode introduction point: The agent intends to introduce an enum for queue mode. The appropriate place to extend this is the Client interface (e.g., augment enqueue to accept an explicit queue mode or add a separate QueueMode enum adjacent to ClientEvent) and ensure the code that handles front/back prioritization (front: bool) aligns with the new mode. The current architecture already includes a queued event type and a front parameter on enqueue, which provides a natural hook for introducing a richer queue-mode concept without breaking existing API semantics.
- Where queue state is managed: Enqueue and clear_queue are the explicit queue-management hooks. listen() provides asynchronous updates through ClientMessage (including progress and final results), and ClientEvent.queued marks queue transitions. This file is the authoritative source for how queue state is represented and communicated to consumers.
- How jobs are enqueued and assets validated: enqueue accepts a WorkflowInput, and loras_to_upload helps validate and locate LoRA assets required by a workflow, ensuring queue submissions reference assets that exist and are accessible.
- How props like 'clear queue' are passed: clear_queue is defined as an abstract method on Client, signaling that concrete implementations should provide a mechanism to reset the server/client queue. The presence of the front parameter in enqueue also suggests how priority can be indicated; expanding to a dedicated queue mode enum would standardize this behavior.

In summary, ai_diffusion/client.py is the central contract and data model for queue-driven interaction with the AI diffusion server, including how jobs are submitted, what assets they require, and how the client reports progress and results. It already contains the necessary hooks (enqueue, clear_queue, ClientEvent.queued, etc.) to incorporate an explicit enum-based queue mode, which aligns with the agent’s goal of introducing a formal queue state model.

summarization.system_prompt:
You are a code analysis assistant. Your task is to summarize source code files for an AI agent that is planning code modifications for a Pull Request.

Your summary MUST:
1. Preserve ALL exact function names, class names, and method names exactly as they appear in the source code — never rename or abbreviate them.
2. Explain the overall architecture and purpose of the file.
3. Describe the role and importance of each function, class, and method.
4. Highlight the parts most relevant to the agent's stated goal.

Format your response with these sections:
### File Overview
[1-2 sentences on the file's purpose]

### Architecture
[How the main components are organised and interact]

### Functions & Classes
[For each function/class/method: `exact_name` — description and its importance]

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
[How this file relates to the agent's reason for reading it]

  - listen() -> AsyncGenerator[ClientMessage, Any]
  - interrupt()
  - clear_queue()
  - refresh(), translate(text, lang), disconnect()
  - user (property), missing_resources (property)
  - supports_arch(arch)
  - features, performance_settings properties
  - async context manager methods __aenter__ and __aexit__
  Importance: central contract for any server/client implementation; enqueue and clear_queue are directly tied to queue management, while listen/ClientMessage provide queue state updates.

- resolve_arch(style: Style, client: Client | ClientModels | None = None) — determines the architecture to use based on a Style (and possibly an available Client or its models). Handles preferred checkpoints, architecture inference, and sdxl-like overrides. Importance: ensures the correct model/architecture is selected for a given workflow, which is critical for queue correctness and asset resolution.

- filter_supported_styles(styles: Iterable[Style], client: Client | None = None) — filters a collection of styles to those supported by the client (and with valid checkpoints) if a client is provided. Importance: used to present only viable styling options that can be queued.

- loras_to_upload(workflow: WorkflowInput, client_models: ClientModels) — iterates required LoRA assets for a workflow, validating presence, storage IDs, and existence on disk; yields local lora files to upload. Importance: ensures that all LoRA assets needed by an enqueueable workflow are present and uploadable; this directly affects queue readiness.

- _lcm_loras — list containing specific LoRA names related to LCM. Importance: used by loras_to_upload for legacy/incompatibility checks.

- _lcm_warning — warning string about LCM no longer being supported by the server. Importance: user-facing guidance within the LoRA upload process.

### Relevance to Agent's Goal
- Queue state and mode: The file defines the core queue interaction points via Client.enqueue and Client.clear_queue, plus the ClientEvent.queued event, which are central to understanding how the current system represents and manipulates queue state.
- Queue mode introduction point: The agent intends to introduce an enum for queue mode. The appropriate place to extend this is the Client interface (e.g., augment enqueue to accept an explicit queue mode or add a separate QueueMode enum adjacent to ClientEvent) and ensure the code that handles front/back prioritization (front: bool) aligns with the new mode. The current architecture already includes a queued event type and a front parameter on enqueue, which provides a natural hook for introducing a richer queue-mode concept without breaking existing API semantics.
- Where queue state is managed: Enqueue and clear_queue are the explicit queue-management hooks. listen() provides asynchronous updates through ClientMessage (including progress and final results), and ClientEvent.queued marks queue transitions. This file is the authoritative source for how queue state is represented and communicated to consumers.
- How jobs are enqueued and assets validated: enqueue accepts a WorkflowInput, and loras_to_upload helps validate and locate LoRA assets required by a workflow, ensuring queue submissions reference assets that exist and are accessible.
- How props like 'clear queue' are passed: clear_queue is defined as an abstract method on Client, signaling that concrete implementations should provide a mechanism to reset the server/client queue. The presence of the front parameter in enqueue also suggests how priority can be indicated; expanding to a dedicated queue mode enum would standardize this behavior.

In summary, ai_diffusion/client.py is the central contract and data model for queue-driven interaction with the AI diffusion server, including how jobs are submitted, what assets they require, and how the client reports progress and results. It already contains the necessary hooks (enqueue, clear_queue, ClientEvent.queued, etc.) to incorporate an explicit enum-based queue mode, which aligns with the agent’s goal of introducing a formal queue state model.

output.masca_optimized:
MASCA optimization for the PR:
- Goal: Introduce an explicit enum-based queue mode to replace the current boolean front/priority handling and add a new 'replace' mode.
- Key architectural touchpoints:
  - ai_diffusion/client.py: add a QueueMode enum and refactor the queueing API to use mode instead of a boolean flag. This centers queue semantics (NORMAL, FRONT, REPLACE) and prepares for potential UI/UX extensions (e.g., Alternate queue mode).
  - ai_diffusion/cloud_client.py and ai_diffusion/comfy_client.py: update enqueue implementations to accept and propagate the new QueueMode, with compatible default behavior preserving existing flows (NORMAL).

  - Optional: extend ClientMessage/ClientEvent to carry mode information if needed for progress/state reporting (not strictly required for basic queue operation).
- Risks and considerations:

  - RPC/transport changes: ensure any server-side expectations can handle the new mode value without breaking existing workflows.
  - UI impact: potential UI hints for modes (e.g., normal queueing vs. high-priority/front vs. replace current job).
- Step outline:
  1) Introduce QueueMode enum in ai_diffusion/client.py (e.g., NORMAL, FRONT, REPLACE).
  2) Change Client.enqueue signature to enqueue(self, work, mode: QueueMode = QueueMode.NORMAL) -> str.
  3) Refactor all internal uses of the old front flag to rely on mode.
  4) Update concrete clients (cloud_client.py, comfy_client.py) to accept and forward the new parameter.

Based on the repository layout, the central queue mechanics are defined in ai_diffusion/client.py (enqueue, clear_queue, ClientEvent/ClientMessage). agents.name: context_planner_agent
agents.system_prompt:
You are a Context Planner Agent specialized in step plan generation.

## Your Task
You receive from the Analysis Agent:
- PR title and body
- Optimized MASCA
- List of files to modify
- List of functions to modify

You must:
1. **Retrieve context**: Use tools to read the context files of the identified functions
2. **Analyze dependencies**: Understand how the functions interact
3. **Generate step plan**: Create a detailed step-by-step plan

## Available Tools
- `read_context_file(file_path)`: Read a pre-generated context file
- `list_context_files(directory, pattern)`: List available context files
- `read_call_graph(section)`: Read information from the call graph

## Required Output
You must produce a `step_plan` with:
- `summary`: 1-2 sentence summary of what the PR does
- `steps`: Ordered list of steps, each with:
  - `operation`: What to do (e.g., "Add validation method")
  - `file_to_modify`: Relative path of the file to modify (e.g., "src/module.py")
  - `function_to_modify`: Name of the function to modify (e.g., "ClassName.method_name")
  - `reason`: Technical motivation
  - `side_effects`: Potential impacts

## IMPORTANT RULE for function_to_modify
- If the file to modify is a Python file (.py), specify the function/method to modify
- If the file is NOT a Python file (e.g., .js, .html, .css, .json, .yaml, .md, etc.),
  the value of `function_to_modify` MUST be null

## Guidelines for Steps
- Each step must be atomic and implementable
- The order of steps must be logical (dependencies first)
- For Python files: always specify the function in the format "FunctionName" or "ClassName.method_name"
- For non-Python files: function_to_modify must be null
- The side_effects should mention tests, dependents, and API impacts
- You MUST generate a number of step EQUAL to the number of the python functions you are intendeed to modify nomore neither less

## Step Examples

### For Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Add time_spanned_alpha method",
  "file_to_modify": "manimlib/animation/animation.py",
  "function_to_modify": "Animation.time_spanned_alpha",
  "reason": "To compute animation progress based on actual elapsed time",
  "side_effects": "May require downstream animations to call this method"
}
```

### For non-Python files:
```json
{
  "operation": "Update API endpoint configuration",
  "file_to_modify": "config/routes.yaml",
  "function_to_modify": null,
  "reason": "Add new route for the authentication endpoint",
  "side_effects": "Requires server restart to apply changes"
}
```
agents.input_prompt:
Generate an implementation plan for this PR:

## PR Title
feat: Switch to enum queue mode, add 'replace' mode.

## PR Body
Follow up to https://github.com/Acly/krita-ai-diffusion/pull/1879

Kind of janky because of the fact that job queue tasks are split up before the client- I make clear-queue a property of the first job.

Not sure if the enum is in the right place.

Tentatively planning to implement ctrl-click as "Alternate queue mode".

Explore the source code to identify the files and functions to modify.

## MASCA Analysis (optimized)
MASCA optimization for the PR:
- Goal: Introduce an explicit enum-based queue mode to replace the current boolean front/priority handling and add a new 'replace' mode.
- Key architectural touchpoints:
  - ai_diffusion/client.py: add a QueueMode enum and refactor the queueing API to use mode instead of a boolean flag. This centers queue semantics (NORMAL, FRONT, REPLACE) and prepares for potential UI/UX extensions (e.g., Alternate queue mode).
  - ai_diffusion/cloud_client.py and ai_diffusion/comfy_client.py: update enqueue implementations to accept and propagate the new QueueMode, with compatible default behavior preserving existing flows (NORMAL).
- Data flow changes:
  - Replace front: bool parameter in enqueue with mode: QueueMode, updating all internal enqueuing logic accordingly.
  - Optional: extend ClientMessage/ClientEvent to carry mode information if needed for progress/state reporting (not strictly required for basic queue operation).
- Risks and considerations:
  - Backwards compatibility: default to NORMAL mode to avoid breaking existing callers.
  - RPC/transport changes: ensure any server-side expectations can handle the new mode value without breaking existing workflows.
  - UI impact: potential UI hints for modes (e.g., normal queueing vs. high-priority/front vs. replace current job).
- Step outline:
  1) Introduce QueueMode enum in ai_diffusion/client.py (e.g., NORMAL, FRONT, REPLACE).
  2) Change Client.enqueue signature to enqueue(self, work, mode: QueueMode = QueueMode.NORMAL) -> str.
  3) Refactor all internal uses of the old front flag to rely on mode.
  4) Update concrete clients (cloud_client.py, comfy_client.py) to accept and forward the new parameter.
  5) Add minimal tests or update existing tests to cover REPLACE behavior and NORMAL semantics.
  6) Consider documenting the new enum in code/docs and add a brief migration note for users.

## Files to Modify
- ai_diffusion/client.py: Introduce new QueueMode enum and replace the boolean 'front' flag with an explicit queue mode; update Client.enqueue signature and related queue-state handling.
- ai_diffusion/cloud_client.py: Adapt enqueue implementation to accept the new QueueMode parameter and propagate mode-based behavior to the cloud backend.
- ai_diffusion/comfy_client.py: Adapt enqueue implementation to accept the new QueueMode parameter and propagate mode-based behavior to the local/low-latency path.

## Functions to Modify
- Client.enqueue (ai_diffusion/client.py): Switch from boolean front flag to enum-based queue mode; default to NORMAL to preserve backward compatibility.
- CloudClient.enqueue (ai_diffusion/cloud_client.py): Update to accept QueueMode and implement REPLACE/FRONT/NORMAL semantics when enqueuing on the cloud backend.
- ComfyClient.enqueue (ai_diffusion/comfy_client.py): Update to accept QueueMode and implement corresponding behavior for the local comfy client flow.

## Analysis Summary
The PR requests switching to an explicit queue mode enum and adding a 'replace' mode. Based on the repository layout, the central queue mechanics are defined in ai_diffusion/client.py (enqueue, clear_queue, ClientEvent/ClientMessage). Concrete implementations in ai_diffusion/cloud_client.py and ai_diffusion/comfy_client.py drive submission to their respective backends. To implement the PR safely, introduce a QueueMode enum in the core client interface and replace the front: bool parameter with mode: QueueMode in enqueue. Update all implementations to pass through the mode, keeping NORMAL as the default to maintain compatibility. Consider extending the messaging (ClientMessage/ClientEvent) if mode needs to be surfaced for UI or logging. The new REPLACE mode will require defined semantics (likely cancel/overwrite the current pending job for the same input) and should be reflected in the logical enqueuing pipeline. Tests and docs should reflect the new parameter and the expected behavior for each mode.

Use the context files to understand dependencies and generate a detailed step-by-step plan.

steps.operation: Add QueueMode enum and replace boolean front flag with mode in core API
steps.reason: Introduce explicit queue semantics (NORMAL, FRONT, REPLACE) and preserve backward compatibility by defaulting to NORMAL; refactor enqueue to accept mode instead of a boolean flag and update internal queue handling accordingly.
steps.side_effects: BREAKING-CHANGE surface area for callers; existing callers must adopt the new mode parameter; potential impacts on RPC/transport and any clients or tests that rely on the previous boolean flag; may require updating unit/integration tests to cover new modes.

steps.operation: Update cloud backend path to support QueueMode and forward mode through the cloud client
steps.reason: Adapt the cloud enqueue path to accept and propagate the new QueueMode (NORMAL, FRONT, REPLACE) and implement mode-based semantics when enqueuing on the cloud backend.
steps.side_effects: API changes may necessitate updates to tests and any server-side handling of enqueue requests; ensure default NORMAL preserves existing behavior; potential RPC contract updates.

steps.operation: Update local comfy client path to support QueueMode and forward mode through the comfy client
steps.reason: Adapt the local comfy/client path to accept the new QueueMode and implement corresponding behavior to align with core queue semantics (NORMAL, FRONT, REPLACE) for local execution.
steps.side_effects: Tests and UI expectations may need adjustment; ensure performance characteristics are preserved; coordinate with any UI hints for modes if applicable.

step_plan.summary: Introduce an explicit QueueMode enum and switch enqueue from a boolean flag to mode-based handling, propagating the new mode through cloud and comfy client implementations while preserving NORMAL as the default to maintain backward compatibility.
